summary: A seven-day (eight outside Israel) Jewish harvest holiday beginning on the 15th of Tishrei, five days after Yom Kippur, during which observers build and dwell in temporary booths (sukkot) roofed with plant matter (s'chach) to commemorate the Israelites' forty years wandering the desert. Central rituals include waving the Four Species (lulav, etrog, hadass, and aravah) and inviting seven symbolic guests, the ushpizin, into the booth each day. It is immediately followed by Shemini Atzeret and then Simchat Torah, and is one of the three biblical pilgrimage festivals (Shalosh Regalim).

works.name: The Sukkah (Booth)
works.description: The temporary, walled booth that gives the holiday its name, built and lived, eaten, and often slept in for the festival's seven days (eight outside Israel). Its roof, the s'chach, must be made of cut plant matter — bamboo, wood, or pine or palm branches, but never straw — symbolizing the divine "clouds of glory" that sheltered the Israelites in the wilderness. The Mishnah's tractate Sukkah governs its construction in detail, and modern sukkot have inspired art and design competitions.

clues.clue: Its roof, called s'chach, must be made of plant matter cut from the ground (bamboo, wood, or pine/palm branches, never straw), symbolizing God's protective "clouds of glory"
examples: These objects include a sechach that must be made of produce cut from the ground to commemorate God's "clouds of glory."
Modern celebrants of this holiday often use bamboo or wood to make a s'khach.

clues.clue: Name these temporary dwellings that give their name to the seven-day harvest festival, commemorating the Israelites' desert wanderings
examples: name these temporary dwellings that provide the name for a seven-day harvest festival in Judaism.
name these structures in which Jews eat, sleep, and celebrate for the seven or eight days of their namesake autumn holiday.

clues.clue: According to Rabbi Yochanan, God will build one for the righteous in the world to come from the shining skin of the Leviathan
examples: According to Rabbi Yochanan, God will redeem the righteous by making one of these objects from Leviathan's shining skin.

clues.clue: 2010 design competitions, including one in New York's Union Square Park ("Sukkah City"), showcased experimental versions of it, with entries in one case required to be built atop a camel
examples: A 2010 design competition in New York's Union Square Park based around this festival's traditions allowed for entries to "be built on top of a camel".
In 2010, hundreds of artist versions of objects used on this holiday were displayed in a New York City art competition.

clues.clue: The Mishnah's tractate on it (sixth in the order of Mo'ed) permits a two-poster but not a four-poster bed inside, and forbids building it atop a pre-existing colonnade
examples: The treatise in the Mishnah named for this is the sixth in the mo'ed order, allows a two-poster bed but not a four-poster bed in this place, and notes that it may not use a preexisting colonnade.

clues.clue: Karaites hold that the aravot nahal (willow branches) should form part of its structure rather than be used inside it; Rabbi Yose says it may be built either bottom-up or top-down
examples: Karaites believe that the aravot nahal should be a part of this rather than used inside of it, while Rabbi Yose says that one of these can be made in either a bottom-up or top-down way.

clues.clue: In Israeli apartment buildings, only the topmost of two stacked versions of this structure is considered valid, leading to offset or terraced balconies
examples: Apartments in Israel often have terraced or offset balconies because only the top one of these is valid if one is built atop another.

clues.clue: A prayer recited within it includes the phrase "tzivanu leishev ba-[this]" ("commanded us to dwell in the sukkah")
examples: Prayers recited in these places include the phrase "tzivanu leishev ba-[this]," while the ushpizin are the seven guests ritually welcomed into them.

clues.clue: One clue notes that, contrary to common assumption, its roof notably does not have to permit starlight to pass through
examples: The structure used on this holiday notably does not have to permit starlight.

works.name: The Four Species (Arba'at HaMinim)
works.description: The set of four plants — the lulav (palm frond), etrog (citron), hadass (myrtle), and aravah (willow) — that Leviticus specifies for the holiday, bound or held together and waved or shaken (traditionally in six directions) during prayers, and used to bless the sukkah.

clues.clue: Consists of the lulav (palm), etrog (citron), hadass (myrtle), and aravah (willow), collectively the Four Species/Four Kinds, waved or shaken during the holiday's prayers
examples: Celebrants of this holiday wave the four species, which include the lulav and etrog.
Participants wave citron, palm, myrtle and willow in six directions during this festival, and those items are known as the Four Kinds.

clues.clue: Leviticus specifies these four plants as associated with the holiday
examples: Leviticus specifies the hadass, aravah, lulav, and etrog as being associated with this holiday.

clues.clue: According to the Mishnah, an etrog is not kosher for use if it is completely round or missing its "nipple" (pitam)
examples: Chapter three of the Mishna states that one fruit used in this holiday is not kosher if it is completely round or if it is missing its nipple.

clues.clue: Two of the species, the aravah and hadass, are said to resemble the mouth and eyes respectively
examples: Two objects sacred to this holiday, which resemble mouths and eyes respectively, are the aravah and the hadass.

clues.clue: Used to bless the sukkah booths during the holiday
examples: The Four Species are used to bless the booths during this holiday, which is coincidentally also known as the Festival of Booths.

works.name: Ushpizin (the Seven Guests)
works.description: A kabbalistically-derived custom of symbolically inviting seven "exalted guests," or ushpizin, into the sukkah — one for each day of the holiday — traditionally the biblical "seven shepherds": Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, Aaron, Joseph, and David. Some communities add or substitute seven corresponding female guests, the ushpizot.

clues.clue: Seven "exalted guests" or ushpizin — traditionally Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, Aaron, Joseph, and David, the "seven shepherds" of Israel — are ritually invited into the sukkah, one per day
examples: Seven guests, or "ushpizin," are invited each day during this holiday.
A prayer said on this holiday addressed to Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, Aaron, Joseph, and David, seven Biblical shepherds, honors guests called ushpizin.

clues.clue: Many Jews decorate the sukkah with a diagram depicting the seven ushpizin
examples: Many Jews decorate these objects with a diagram showing the ushpizin, a set of seven Biblical patriarchs.

clues.clue: Some communities instead or additionally invoke seven female guests (ushpizot) over the seven days
examples: In some groups, the celebration of this holiday involves the invocation of seven women over the course of seven successive days.
Some observers of this holiday recite the ushpizin prayer, symbolically welcoming seven female guests along with the seven shepherds of Israel.

works.name: Chol HaMoed and Hoshana Rabbah
works.description: The five (six outside Israel) intermediate days of the festival, called Chol HaMoed, during which the Ya'aleh v'Yavo prayer is recited; they culminate in the seventh day, Hoshana Rabbah, marked by circling the synagogue seven times with the lulav and etrog and beating the ground with willow (aravah) branches.

clues.clue: The five (or six, outside Israel) intermediate days of the holiday are called Chol HaMoed, during which the Ya'aleh v'Yavo prayer is recited
examples: the five middle days of this holiday are the Chol Ha'Moed.
Although it is not Passover, the Ya'aleh v'Yavo prayer is recited on its days referred to as Chol ha-Mo'ed.

clues.clue: On the final day, Hoshana Rabbah, worshippers circle the synagogue seven times carrying the lulav and etrog, then beat the ground with willow (aravah) branches
examples: On final day of this holiday, Hoshana Rabbah, observers beat the ground with five willow branches after circling a synagogue seven times with a lulav and etrog.
The aravah is used on the last day of this holiday, during which the synagogue is circled seven times.

works.name: Simchat Beit HaSho'evah (the Water-Drawing Celebration)
works.description: A joyous nightly celebration held during the intermediate days, historically centered on a water-libation ceremony at the Temple; involves music, dancing, and, in ancient practice, pilgrimage rituals connected to Jerusalem's Pool of Siloam.

clues.clue: Music and dancing commemorate a water-drawing ceremony, Simchat Beit HaSho'evah ("Rejoicing of the Place of Water-Drawing"), during the intermediate days
examples: This holiday is sometimes celebrated with music and dance to commemorate a water-drawing ceremony.
Pilgrimage rituals at the Pool of Siloam are celebrated in the "Rejoicing of the Water-Drawing House" during this holiday.

clues.clue: A golden vessel was historically used to collect the water libations poured during the ceremony
examples: A golden vessel was originally used to collect the libations poured during the "Rejoicing of the Water-Drawing House" performed during some of this holiday's intermediate days.

clues.clue: A reception is held in the sukkah during this celebration
examples: A reception is held in this place during Simchat Beit ha-Sho'evah.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Standard closing/identifying clues for Sukkot as a whole, plus scriptural, historical, and calendrical facts that did not cluster into a larger section.

clues.clue: Jewish autumn harvest holiday during which people build and live, eat, or sleep in namesake booths; also called the Feast of Tabernacles, Feast of Booths, or Feast of Ingathering
examples: name this Jewish holiday that follows Yom Kippur, also known as the Feast of the Tabernacles.
name this week-long Jewish autumn holiday, whose observers build and live in outdoor wooden booths.

clues.clue: Begins on the 15th of Tishrei, five days after Yom Kippur, and lasts seven days (eight outside Israel)
examples: This festival is celebrated on the 15th of Tishrei.
This holiday begins five days after Yom Kippur and, unlike Passover, it includes six days of Chol haMoed.

clues.clue: Immediately followed by Shemini Atzeret and then Simchat Torah
examples: This festival is immediately followed by Shemini Atzeret.
Immediately following this festival is the holiday of Simchat Torah.

clues.clue: The transition into Shemini Atzeret features the rain prayer Geshem, recited while wearing a white robe
examples: At the end of this holiday, a white robe is put on to sing the prayer geshem.
This holiday is followed by a day on which Geshem is recited, Shemini Atzeret.

clues.clue: Commemorates the Israelites' forty years wandering the desert after the Exodus, sheltered by God's protective "clouds of glory"
examples: This holiday remembers the Israelites wandering in the desert for 40 years, and it involves waving fronds made of the Four Species.
This event was created as a reminder of the makeshift living conditions Israelites endured after their flight from Egypt.

clues.clue: One of the Shalosh Regalim (the three biblical pilgrimage festivals), the first celebrated after Rosh Hashanah
examples: On its religion's calendar, this holiday is the first of the Shalosh Regalim each year.
Samaritans commemorate this holiday by building a trellis and going to Mount Gezerim, and it is the first pilgrimage festival after Rosh Hashanah.

clues.clue: Hallel (Psalms 113-118) is recited during the holiday, as on several others
examples: Psalms 113-118 form the text of the Hallel prayer, which is recited during many Jewish holidays, including this one, the "Festival of Booths" that begins five days after Yom Kippur.

clues.clue: The entire Book of Psalms is read on one day of the holiday, when celebrants greet each other with "pitka tava"
examples: On one day of this holiday, people typically greet each other with pitka tava and the entire book of Psalms is read.

clues.clue: Moses established the 15th of the seventh month as the holiday's start date; Exodus 34 refers to it as the "Feast of Ingathering"
examples: Moses established this holiday's start date as the fifteenth day of the seventh month.
This "Feast of Ingathering" is described in Exodus 34.

clues.clue: Jeroboam instituted a false/perverted version of the holiday at an altar in Bethel in the northern Kingdom of Israel
examples: An altar at Bethel was set up for a false version of this holiday promoted by Jeroboam.
Jeroboam created a perverted version of this holiday in the northern Kingdom.

clues.clue: Ezra revived the holiday's celebration by reading the Law at the Water Gate, and it was the first holiday observed in Jerusalem after Temple sacrifices resumed following the Babylonian Captivity; Nehemiah later instituted its observance after completing the city's walls
examples: Ezra read from the book of the Law at the Water Gate before reviving the celebration of this holiday.
After completing the walls of Jerusalem, Nehemiah institutes this Feast of Booths, which modern Jews celebrate every 15th of Tishrei.

clues.clue: The prophet Zechariah (ch. 14) warns that Gentiles who refuse to celebrate the holiday in the eschaton will suffer drought and a flesh-rotting plague
examples: According to Zechariah 14, drought and horrific flesh-rotting plague will be the fate of Gentiles that refuse to participate in eschatological celebrations of this holiday.

clues.clue: In the New Testament, Paul left Ephesus to observe this "feast of tabernacles," and Jesus proclaims that "rivers of living water" would flow from believers on the holiday's final day (Gospel of John)
examples: Paul left Ephesus to partake in this "feast of tabernacles."

During the final day of one occurence of this festival, Jesus cried out "Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink," before promising that "rivers of living water" would flow out of whoever believed in him.

clues.clue: Scholar Johannes C. de Moor has posited a connection between the holiday and the Ugaritic New Year
examples: Johannes C. de Moor has posited a connection between this holiday and the Ugaritic New Year.

clues.clue: Once every seven years, the king publicly read the Torah during the festival, in a practice called Hakhel
examples: Once every seven years, the king reads the Torah during this festival in a tradition known as Hakhel.

clues.clue: According to 2 Maccabees, the "first Hanukkah" celebrated by the Maccabees was actually a delayed observance of this festival
examples: The "first Hanukkah" celebrated by the Maccabees was, according to 2 Maccabees, in fact a delayed observance of this festival, which heavily involves the hadass, aravah, lulav, and etrog.

clues.clue: The obligation to bring first fruits (bikkurim) to the Temple, beginning at Shavuot, concluded at this festival
examples: The Israelite obligation to proffer up the first fruits of the harvest began with the festival of Shavuot and ended with this seven-day "feast of booths."

clues.clue: Also known as Z'man Simchateinu ("Season of Rejoicing"); contains the last of the "Days of Judgment" in the Jewish liturgical calendar
examples: This holiday is occasionally called the Season of Rejoicing, or Z'man Simchateinu.
This holiday contains the last of the Days of Judgement.

comprehensive_summary: Sukkot ("Booths," also called the Feast of Tabernacles, Feast of Booths, or Feast of Ingathering) is a seven-day Jewish harvest festival beginning on the 15th of Tishrei, five days after Yom Kippur, and one of the three biblical pilgrimage festivals (Shalosh Regalim). Its central practice is dwelling in a sukkah, a temporary booth roofed with cut plant matter (s'chach) — never straw — symbolizing the divine "clouds of glory" that sheltered the Israelites during their forty years wandering the desert after the Exodus; the Mishnah's tractate Sukkah governs its construction in detail (down to permissible bed sizes and the invalidity of a booth built atop another), and the structure has inspired modern art and design competitions such as 2010's Union Square Park "Sukkah City." Celebrants wave the Four Species — the lulav (palm), etrog (citron), hadass (myrtle), and aravah (willow) specified in Leviticus — and each day symbolically invite seven "exalted guests," the ushpizin (traditionally Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, Aaron, Joseph, and David, the "seven shepherds"; some communities add female ushpizot), often depicted on a decorative diagram in the sukkah. The holiday's five or six intermediate days are called Chol HaMoed, during which the Ya'aleh v'Yavo prayer is recited; they culminate in Hoshana Rabbah, when worshippers circle the synagogue seven times with the lulav and etrog and beat the ground with willow branches. Ancient celebrations also featured Simchat Beit HaSho'evah, a water-drawing festival with music, dancing, and Temple libation rituals tied to the Pool of Siloam. Sukkot has a dense biblical and historical pedigree: Moses is said to have established its date, Exodus 34 calls it the "Feast of Ingathering," Jeroboam instituted a rival version in the northern Kingdom at Bethel, Ezra revived its observance by reading the Law at the Water Gate after the Babylonian Captivity, and Nehemiah reinstituted it after rebuilding Jerusalem's walls. The prophet Zechariah gives it eschatological significance, threatening Gentiles who refuse to celebrate it with plague, while the New Testament depicts Paul observing it and Jesus proclaiming "rivers of living water" during its final day. According to 2 Maccabees, the first Hanukkah was itself a delayed Sukkot observance. The holiday is immediately followed by Shemini Atzeret (marked by the rain prayer Geshem) and then Simchat Torah.
recursive_suggestions: Shemini Atzeret — immediately follows Sukkot and has its own bonus answerlines in this corpus (2013 DRAGOON, 2013 ACF Regionals); deserves its own page and cross-link.
Simchat Torah — the Torah-completion ceremony following Sukkot/Shemini Atzeret, with its own bonus answerline (2012 ACF Nationals).

The Four Species — appeared as its own bonus answerline twice (2012 ACF Regionals, 2012 ACF Nationals) distinct from Sukkot; could merit a standalone page if a future set clues it heavily outside the Sukkot context.
Hakhel — the septennial public Torah reading has its own bonus answerline (2012 ACF Regionals); currently thin but worth watching.
Nehemiah — separate Bible/History figure and book with his own bonus answerline (2013 WIT); the wall-rebuilding and Sukkot-instituting narrative should cross-link.
Ushpizin (2004 film) — the bonus about Shuli Rand (2014 VCU Open) is about a film named for the ushpizin prayer; flag for possible Film/VFA category overlap if it accumulates more clues.
